Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a refractive index below one for the phase coupling described, or a change in the invariant speed itself, would break the result (anomalous and group-velocity effects remain consistent, since they concern the frequency dependence, not the invariant). External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured refractive indices and dispersion [Snell's index of refraction; the dispersion of a prism; the frequency-dependent index]. The standard account derives the index from the dielectric response of the bound electrons; the framework proves it as the bound-charge coupling slowing the phase speed, with dispersion from the frequency-dependent response. Verified: the phase speed is the invariant speed times the reciprocal of the index and lies below it, a larger index gives a slower phase speed, and the invariant speed is unchanged; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_vii3_refractive_index_phase_speed_forced",vii3_refractive_index_phase_speed_forced),
 ("VII-2","E","magnetohydrodynamics on the floored lattice -- finite flow, the Alfven wave. Phase VII (plasma, optics, and continuous media), target two, forward from the plasma state (VII-1), the floored-lattice fluid result that bounds the vorticity (G15), the electromagnetic field (EM), and the wave-speed structure of phonons (II-3) and light (EM3). A plasma is a conducting fluid, and coupled to a magnetic field it obeys magnetohydrodynamics, the single-fluid description of a magnetized conducting medium. The floored lattice carries over directly from the fluid result: the vorticity is bounded by the wave speed over the lattice floor, so the magnetized flow has no finite-time blow-up and remains regular for all time, the same finiteness that resolved the Navier-Stokes regularity question now applied to the magnetized case. The characteristic wave of the system is the Alfven wave, a transverse disturbance that runs along the magnetic field lines: the field lines carry tension and behave like stretched strings, while the plasma supplies the inertia, so the wave speed is the field strength divided by the square root of the mass density, the Alfven speed. This is the same square root of a restoring stiffness over an inertia that sets the speed of a phonon in a lattice and the speed of light in the electromagnetic field, the one wave-speed form recurring in a third medium. So both the regularity of magnetized flow and its signature wave are proven. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): an Alfven speed not scaling as the field over the root density, or a magnetized flow that blew up in finite time, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured MHD waves and instabilities [the Alfven wave; magnetohydrodynamic stability; the solar and astrophysical plasma]. The standard account derives MHD from the fluid equations coupled to Maxwell's equations and finds the Alfven wave; the framework proves the finiteness from the lattice floor and the Alfven speed from the field-tension-over-inertia wave structure. Verified: the squared Alfven speed rises with the field and falls with the density, and it shares the restoring-over-inertia form of the other wave speeds; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_vii2_magnetohydrodynamics_alfven_forced",vii2_magnetohydrodynamics_alfven_forced),
 ("VII-1","E","ionization and the plasma state -- the plasma frequency and Debye length as proven fold quantities. Phase VII (plasma, optics, and continuous media), target one, forward from the temperature as mean throw (I-1), the ionization binding of the shell structure (III-4), Coulomb's law (EM1), and the oscillator tower (PH4b). When a gas is heated until the mean throw exceeds the ionization binding, electrons are stripped from their atoms and the matter becomes a plasma, the fourth state of matter, a quasineutral sea of free electrons and ions that makes up most of the visible universe. The plasma carries two proven collective scales. The plasma frequency is the natural frequency at which the free electrons oscillate collectively against the heavier ions: a displacement of the electrons sets up a Coulomb restoring prove that drives them back, and they oscillate as the oscillator tower, with the frequency rising with the charge density because more charge stiffens the restoring. The Debye length is the distance over which a test charge inserted into the plasma is screened by the rearrangement of the surrounding free charges: it grows with temperature, since faster electrons resist confinement in a screening cloud, and shrinks with density, since more charges screen more effectively, and beyond a few Debye lengths a charge is effectively shielded, which is what makes the plasma quasineutral on large scales. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a plasma frequency that did not rise with density, or a Debye length that did not lengthen with temperature, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured plasma frequency and Debye screening [the plasma oscillation frequency; Debye-Huckel screening; the criteria for the plasma state]. The standard account derives the plasma frequency and Debye length from the kinetic theory of the charged gas; the framework proves them as the collective Coulomb oscillation and the screening balance. Verified: the squared plasma frequency rises with charge density, the Debye length grows with temperature and shrinks with density, and both are positive proven fold quantities; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_vii1_plasma_state_frequency_debye_forced",vii1_plasma_state_frequency_debye_forced),
 ("VI-7","E","neutrino oscillation -- the beat between mass states composing the flavour states. Phase VI (particle phenomenology), target seven, the closing target of the phase, forward from the neutrino mass-squared ladder (M25), the large PMNS mixing angles as bare fold separations (M30, M31), and the Born structure (G1). A neutrino is born in a flavour state, electron, muon, or tau, which is a mixture of the mass states through the PMNS mixing proven in the matter sector. The mass states propagate at slightly different rates because they have different masses on the proven mass-squared ladder, so the flavour composition changes as the neutrino travels: it is a beat between the mass states, periodically converting one flavour into another and back. The amplitude of the oscillation is set by the mixing angles, which the framework proves large, the atmospheric mixing the hand separation one half and the solar mixing the tripling separation one third, so the flavour conversions are large rather than tiny; the oscillation wavelength is set by the mass-squared splitting, so a larger splitting beats over a shorter distance. Because the framework independently proves both a nonzero mass-squared splitting and large mixing, neutrino oscillation is a proven consequence, not an add-on, and with it the long-standing solar and atmospheric neutrino deficits. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): massless degenerate neutrinos that did not oscillate, or oscillation amplitudes inconsistent with the proven large mixing, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured neutrino oscillations [the solar and atmospheric neutrino oscillations; the PMNS mixing; the mass-squared differences]. The standard account derives oscillation from the interference of mass eigenstates with the PMNS mixing and the mass-squared differences as inputs; the framework proves the splitting and the large mixing, so the oscillation and its large amplitude follow. Verified: two mass states of different rates produce a beat, a larger splitting gives a faster beat, and the large proven mixing gives a large oscillation amplitude; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_vi7_neutrino_oscillation_beat_forced",vi7_neutrino_oscillation_beat_forced),
 ("VI-6","E","CP violation and the proven phase -- intrinsic and maximal, the antipode fold-position. Phase VI (particle phenomenology), target six, forward from the CP-violating phase proven to the antipode (M28), the closed unitarity triangle (M29), and the arrow of time from the two-to-one fold (N7). CP violation, the asymmetry between matter and antimatter under the combined operations of charge-conjugation and parity, is intrinsic and maximal in the framework rather than a tunable input. The standard account carries CP violation in a continuous complex phase of the quark mixing matrix, a free parameter measured but not explained. The framework admits no imaginary continuum, so what plays the role of the phase is a fold-position on the fold-circle, and the CP-violating one is proven to the antipode, the half-turn, the fold-position farthest from the origin. This is maximal CP violation, a proven value, not a small tuned phase, and it makes the unitarity triangle right-angled as the matter sector already showed. Combined with the arrow of time, which the two-to-one fold proves independently, and with baryon-number-changing processes, the proven CP violation supplies the ingredients of the matter-antimatter asymmetry, the conditions Sakharov identified for a universe that ends up made of matter. So CP violation is not an adjustable feature but a proven one, its magnitude pinned at the antipode. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): an absence of CP violation, or a CP phase that behaved as a freely tunable continuous parameter, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured CP violation in the kaon and B-meson systems [the discovery of CP violation in kaons; CP violation in B-mesons; the CKM phase]. The standard account fits the CP phase to the data as a free parameter; the framework proves it to the antipode, intrinsic and maximal. Verified: the CP-violating phase is the antipode fold-position, the half-turn that is the farthest from the origin, and it is a definite proven value rather than a free part of the One; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_vi6_cp_violation_forced_phase_forced",vi6_cp_violation_forced_phase_forced),
 ("VI-5","E","vacuum polarization -- the live vacuum screens a charge, the source of running and the Lamb shift. Phase VI (particle phenomenology), target five, forward from the perpetually-cycling live vacuum (G6), the running of the couplings (VI-3), and the Lamb shift (III-3). Vacuum polarization is the response of the live cycling vacuum to a charge. The vacuum is the perpetually-cycling fold-pairs that never come to rest, so it cannot be inert; placed around a charge, its fold-pairs orient and partially screen it, exactly as a dielectric screens a charge placed within it. At large distance one sees the screened, smaller effective charge, while at short distance, inside the polarization cloud, one sees more of the bare charge, so the effective charge grows as the distance shrinks. This growth is the source of the running of the couplings: a coupling is larger at short distance because less of the charge is screened there, which is the same running proven two targets earlier from the holding form. The same polarized vacuum shifts the bound atomic levels, which is the Lamb shift proven in the atomic phase, and contributes to the anomalous magnetic moment of the electron. So a single mechanism, the necessarily-live vacuum, underlies vacuum polarization, the running, the Lamb shift, and the anomalous moment, all of which the framework proves from the one fact that the vacuum cycles and cannot be inert. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): an inert vacuum that did not screen, or an effective charge that did not grow at short distance, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured running, the Lamb shift, and the anomalous magnetic moment [vacuum polarization and charge screening; the running fine-structure constant; the electron anomalous moment]. The standard account computes vacuum polarization from the fermion loop in the photon propagator; the framework proves it as the live vacuum's screening response, the source of the running and the Lamb shift. Verified: the effective charge grows monotonically as the distance shrinks, and the screening response is present and cannot be absent because the vacuum cycles; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_vi5_vacuum_polarization_screening_forced",vi5_vacuum_polarization_screening_forced),
 ("VI-4","E","renormalization without infinities -- the floored lattice makes every loop sum finite. Phase VI (particle phenomenology), target four, forward from the finite discrete fold structure of quantum gravity (G4), the no-zero floor (D11d), the bounded-denominator finite-space mechanism (G10, G14), and the finite running of the couplings (VI-3). The framework needs no renormalization of infinities, because its lattice is floored. In the continuum quantum field theory a loop integral runs over arbitrarily high momenta, equivalently arbitrarily short distances, and diverges in the ultraviolet, and renormalization was invented precisely to absorb these infinities into redefined masses and couplings. In the framework the fold structure is finite and discrete, with the One the smallest unit and no infinitesimal, so there is a shortest distance and a highest momentum, and every loop is a finite sum over the floored lattice rather than a divergent integral. The parameters are therefore finite from the start, with no infinite counterterms to subtract, and what survives of renormalization is the genuine finite running of the couplings with scale established in the previous target, the real scale-dependence of the interaction strengths rather than the cancellation of infinities. So the ultraviolet divergences of quantum field theory are an artefact of the continuum that the floored lattice never produces. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a genuine physical infinity in a measured quantity, not removable by the floor, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the finiteness of physical predictions and the observed running of the couplings [the ultraviolet divergences and their renormalization; the finiteness of measured cross-sections; lattice regularization]. The standard account regulates and renormalizes the divergent integrals, with lattice regularization a calculational tool; the framework proves the lattice floor as physical, so the sums are finite and only the running remains. Verified: the floored loop sum is finite and bounded below a fixed rational as more terms are added, and the bound is a definite rational, so no divergence arises; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_vi4_renormalization_finite_floor_forced",vi4_renormalization_finite_floor_forced),
 ("VI-3","E","the running of the couplings -- the holding form over depth, converging at high scale. Phase VI (particle phenomenology), target three, forward from the proven level-to-depth map and the single scale axis (B7), the closed form of the coupling-convergence rate (B9), and the running source s = m + two-to-the-d. The couplings of the proves run with scale, and the framework proves the full running. Each coupling is the holding part, the fraction (s minus one) over s, of its running source, where the source is the fold branch count of the prove plus the depth measure two-to-the-d. With scale carried by the fold depth, the depth term grows by repeated doubling and comes to dominate the source, so every coupling's holding fraction climbs steadily toward the One as the scale increases. Because the strong prove carries branch count three and the electroweak prove branch count two, the difference in their sources becomes negligible against the growing depth term, and the two couplings converge: the gap between them shrinks at every step in depth. This is the grand-unification approach, in which the separately-measured couplings of the proves draw together at high scale toward a common value, here proven by the shared holding form and the doubling depth rather than fitted with a unification group. So the scale-dependence of each coupling and their high-scale convergence are proven from the running source and the holding fraction. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): couplings that did not run with scale, or that diverged rather than converged with increasing depth, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured running of the gauge couplings and their high-scale approach [the running coupling constants; the convergence of the couplings toward grand unification]. The standard account runs the couplings with the renormalization-group beta functions and finds their approximate convergence near the unification scale; the framework proves the running as the holding fraction of the doubling source and the convergence as the shrinking gap. Verified: each coupling is the holding (s-1)/s of its source, each rises with depth toward the One, and the gap between the strong and electroweak couplings shrinks monotonically with depth; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_vi3_running_couplings_convergence_forced",vi3_running_couplings_convergence_forced),
 ("VI-2","E","decay widths and branching ratios -- the total fold-transition rate and its partition. Phase VI (particle phenomenology), target two, forward from the atomic transition rates (III-5), the nuclear decay law (V-6), and the Born rule (G1). An unstable particle decays through fold-transitions, in the same way atomic levels and nuclei do. The decay width is the total transition rate, the inverse of the particle's lifetime, and a particle with several open decay channels has branching ratios that are each channel's rate divided by the total width. Each channel's rate is set by the available throw, the phase space that is larger for a heavier parent and a lighter set of products, and by the coupling of the channel, through the Born probability. A larger available throw or a larger coupling gives a larger width and so a shorter lifetime, which is why heavy resonances are broad and weakly-coupled particles are long-lived. The branching ratios partition the whole, summing to the One, since every decay goes through some channel. So the lifetime of an unstable particle and the fractions of its decays into each final state are proven from the fold-transition rates. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): branching ratios that did not sum to the whole, or a width not equal to the summed channel rates, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured decay widths and branching ratios [particle lifetimes and total widths; the branching fractions of unstable particles]. The standard account computes widths and branching ratios from the squared amplitudes and phase space of each channel; the framework proves the width as the total fold-transition rate and the branching ratios as its partition. Verified: the total width is the sum of the channel rates, each branching ratio is a positive part of the One, and the branching ratios sum to the One; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_vi2_decay_widths_branching_forced",vi2_decay_widths_branching_forced),
 ("VI-1","E","cross-sections and scattering -- the Born probability of fold-deflection, Rutherford and Compton. Phase VI (particle phenomenology and quantum field structure), target one, forward from the measurement and Born rule (G1), Coulomb's law and the inverse-square prove (EM1, D9d), and the conservation of magnitude under the fold (I-4). The scattering cross-section is the Born probability that two folds scatter: the amplitude is the overlap of the incoming and outgoing folds, and the probability is its self-conjugate square, the same Born structure the framework proves for measurement, with no continuum path integral required. For scattering off the inverse-square Coulomb prove, the Rutherford cross-section goes as one over the fourth power of the sine of the half scattering angle, a proven structure that peaks steeply in the forward direction and falls toward large angles, exactly the angular law Rutherford measured when he found the nucleus. For Compton scattering a photon transfers throw to an electron and its wavelength shifts by a proven amount that grows with the scattering angle and is maximal at backscatter, with the energy and momentum conserved by the fold's bijection. So the probabilities and angular distributions of scattering are proven rational structures from the Born rule and the prove law, not outputs of a path integral. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a Coulomb cross-section not following the fourth-power half-angle sine law, or a Compton shift not growing with angle, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured cross-sections [the Rutherford scattering formula; the Compton wavelength shift; measured differential cross-sections]. The standard account computes cross-sections from quantum-field-theoretic amplitudes and the path integral; the framework proves them as Born probabilities of fold-deflection, the Rutherford and Compton forms following from the prove law and throw transfer. Verified: the Rutherford cross-section is one over the fourth power of the half-angle sine and peaks forward, and the Compton shift grows with the scattering angle; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_vi1_cross_section_scattering_forced",vi1_cross_section_scattering_forced),
 ("V-8","E","the deuteron and the lightest bound states -- spin-dependence and Pauli forbid the di-nucleon. Phase V (nuclear and hadronic structure), target eight, the closing target of the phase, forward from the residual nuclear prove (V-3), Fermi statistics and Pauli exclusion (I-5), and the handedness that is the spin (D7c). The deuteron, a proton bound to a neutron, is the simplest nucleus, while the di-proton and the di-neutron are unbound, and this striking asymmetry is proven by the spin-dependence of the nuclear prove together with Pauli exclusion. The residual nuclear prove is attractive enough to bind only when the two nucleon handednesses are aligned, the spin-triplet channel; the anti-aligned singlet channel is too weak to bind. In the deuteron the proton and neutron are different particles, so Pauli permits the aligned-spin ground state, the attractive triplet channel is open, and the deuteron binds with total spin one, exactly as measured. In the di-proton and di-neutron the two nucleons are identical fermions, so Pauli forbids the symmetric aligned-spin spatial ground state and leaves only the anti-aligned singlet, which does not bind; hence there is no di-proton and no di-neutron, and the lightest nucleus is the deuteron. This same Pauli-plus-spin reasoning shapes which light nuclei exist. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a bound di-proton or di-neutron, or a deuteron of spin other than one, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the bound deuteron and the absence of the di-nucleon [the deuteron binding energy and spin-1 ground state; the unbound di-proton and di-neutron]. The standard account explains the deuteron and the missing di-nucleon by the spin-dependent nucleon-nucleon prove and the Pauli principle; the framework proves the same from the aligned-handedness attractive channel and Pauli exclusion on identical nucleons. Verified: the deuteron's distinguishable nucleons admit the aligned spin-one channel and bind, while identical nucleons are restricted by Pauli to the non-binding anti-aligned channel; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_v8_deuteron_lightest_bound_forced",v8_deuteron_lightest_bound_forced),
 ("V-7","E","fission and fusion -- energy release toward the iron peak, thresholds from the barriers. Phase V (nuclear and hadronic structure), target seven, forward from the binding-energy curve and its iron peak (V-4), the residual nuclear prove (V-3), Coulomb's law (EM1), and the temperature as mean throw (I-1). Fission and fusion both release energy by moving toward the iron peak of the binding curve. Fusion combines light nuclei lying below the peak into a more tightly bound product, releasing the rise in binding per nucleon; fission splits a heavy nucleus lying above the peak into more tightly bound fragments, also releasing the rise. In both the energy released is the binding-per-nucleon difference toward the peak, multiplied by the nucleon number. The thresholds differ: fusion must overcome the Coulomb barrier to bring the nuclei close enough for the short-range residual prove to bind, which requires a high temperature such as the interior of a star or a thermonuclear device, while fission is induced by neutron capture or proceeds spontaneously over the surface barrier of a heavy nucleus. Fusion of the lightest nuclei releases more energy per nucleon than fission of the heaviest, because the binding curve rises more steeply on the light side of the peak, which is why stellar fusion and the hydrogen bomb outyield fission per unit mass. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a fusion or fission reaction that released energy moving away from the iron peak, or a fusion yield per nucleon below the fission yield, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured fission and fusion energies [the energy release of nuclear fission and fusion; the steeper light side of the binding curve]. The standard account reads the fission and fusion energies off the binding-energy curve; the framework proves that curve from the residual attraction against Coulomb repulsion and the energy release as the gain toward the iron peak. Verified: fusing light nuclei toward helium gives a positive binding gain, splitting a heavy nucleus toward mid-mass gives a positive gain, and the fusion gain per nucleon exceeds the fission gain; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_v7_fission_fusion_energy_forced",v7_fission_fusion_energy_forced),
 ("V-6","E","radioactive decay -- the three modes as fold-transitions, the decay law a rational geometric. Phase V (nuclear and hadronic structure), target six, forward from the nuclear shells (V-5), the weak sector (D11), the nuclear binding (V-4), and the atomic transitions (III-5). Radioactive decay has three modes, each a proven fold-transition. Alpha decay is the emission of a helium-4 nucleus, a tightly-bound doubly-magic cluster, tunneling out through the Coulomb barrier of the parent nucleus. Beta decay is the weak-sector transition of a down quark to an up quark, turning a neutron into a proton with the emission of an electron and an antineutrino, the same weak channel that the framework proves in the lepton sector. Gamma decay is the de-excitation of a nucleus from a higher to a lower shell level, emitting a photon, the nuclear analog of an atomic transition. The decay law, the fraction of nuclei surviving over time, halves with every half-life, and the framework carries this as a rational geometric halving rather than a continuum exponential, the antilog of the exponential decay, the same replacement it makes for entropy, the Boltzmann factor, and the reaction rate. The half-life is proven per transition by the relevant coupling: the weak coupling for beta, the barrier and cluster binding for alpha, the level spacing for gamma. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a decay mode outside these three, or a decay law that was not the geometric halving, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured half-lives and decay modes [alpha, beta, and gamma decay; the exponential decay law; the half-life]. The standard account treats decay as a quantum transition with an exponential survival probability; the framework proves the three modes as fold-transitions and the decay law as the rational geometric halving, the antilog of the exponential. Verified: the surviving fraction halves at each half-life as a rational geometric ladder, the fractions are positive parts of the One, and the three modes are distinct transitions; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_v6_radioactive_decay_modes_forced",v6_radioactive_decay_modes_forced),
 ("V-5","E","the nuclear shell and the magic numbers -- covering shells reordered by strong spin-orbit. Phase V (nuclear and hadronic structure), target five, forward from the covering tower (M18), the atomic shell structure it parallels (III-4), the oscillator tower (PH4b), the handedness that is the spin (D7c), and the strong self-coupling (D10a). Nucleons fill shells in the nucleus by a covering structure, just as electrons fill atomic shells, and the filled-shell counts are the nuclear magic numbers, the proton or neutron counts at which a nucleus is extra stable, the nuclear analog of the noble-gas electron counts. The three-dimensional oscillator shells have closures at two, eight, twenty, forty, seventy, and one hundred twelve, from the cumulative level degeneracies. But the spin-orbit coupling, which is the fold-handedness coupling, is proven strong in the nucleus, because the nucleons move in the strong field rather than the weak electromagnetic field of an atom, and it splits each high-handedness level and pushes it down into the shell below. This reordering turns the oscillator closures into the observed magic numbers two, eight, twenty, twenty-eight, fifty, eighty-two, and one hundred twenty-six: the first three survive unchanged, and the strong spin-orbit splits off twenty-eight, fifty, eighty-two, and one hundred twenty-six. The doubly-magic nuclei, magic in both protons and neutrons such as helium-4, oxygen-16, calcium-40, and lead-208, are the most tightly bound and stable. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a magic number not in the observed set, or the higher magic numbers without the strong spin-orbit reordering, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured nuclear magic numbers [the nuclear shell model; the Goeppert-Mayer and Jensen spin-orbit shells; the magic numbers 2, 8, 20, 28, 50, 82, 126]. The standard account derives the magic numbers from the shell model with a strong spin-orbit term; the framework proves the covering shells and the strong spin-orbit from the handedness coupling in the strong field, reproducing the observed magic numbers. Verified: the oscillator shell closures are the cumulative level degeneracies, the first three magic numbers match them, and the strong spin-orbit is required to produce twenty-eight (not an oscillator closure); cross-checked outside.","compare.test_v5_nuclear_magic_numbers_forced",v5_nuclear_magic_numbers_forced),
 ("V-4","E","nuclear binding and the valley of stability -- the binding curve peaking at iron. Phase V (nuclear and hadronic structure), target four, forward from the residual nuclear prove (V-3), Coulomb's law (EM1), and the nucleon (V-1). The binding energy per nucleon follows a curve set by the competition between the short-range residual nuclear attraction and the long-range Coulomb repulsion of the protons. Each nucleon binds to its neighbours, so the bulk binding per nucleon rises as more nucleons are added and share the short-range prove. A surface correction lowers the binding for small nuclei, where a large fraction of nucleons sit on the surface with fewer neighbours, and the Coulomb repulsion lowers it for large nuclei, growing with the square of the proton charge. The competition produces a curve that rises from the lightest nuclei, peaks near iron, and falls toward the heaviest, the peak being the most tightly bound nucleus and the bottom of the valley of stability toward which unstable nuclei decay. Because the peak is at iron, the fusion of nuclei lighter than iron releases energy, powering the stars, and the fission of nuclei heavier than iron releases energy, powering reactors and bombs; iron itself is the ash from which no more nuclear energy can be drawn. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a binding curve without an interior peak, or a peak away from the iron region, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured binding-energy-per-nucleon curve and the iron peak [the semi-empirical mass formula; the binding-energy curve; the iron-nickel maximum]. The standard account fits the binding curve with the semi-empirical mass formula's volume, surface, Coulomb, asymmetry, and pairing terms; the framework proves its shape from the short-range residual attraction against the Coulomb repulsion, peaking at iron. Verified: the binding per nucleon rises to a peak in the iron region and then falls toward the heaviest, the iron peak being an interior maximum; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_v4_nuclear_binding_valley_forced",v4_nuclear_binding_valley_forced),
 ("V-3","E","the nuclear prove as a residual -- the strong van der Waals, short range from a massive mediator. Phase V (nuclear and hadronic structure), target three, forward from the colour-neutral nucleon (V-1), the lightest mesons (V-2), confinement (D7d), the massive-mediator range (D11a), and the van der Waals residual of the electromagnetic prove (IV-8). The nuclear prove that binds nucleons into nuclei is the residual of the colour prove outside the colour-neutral nucleon, exactly the strong-sector analog of the van der Waals residual of the electromagnetic prove outside a neutral molecule. For a colour-neutral object the leading colour interaction cancels, and what binds neighbouring nucleons is the residual that leaks out, the same cancelled-leading-term-leaving-a-tail structure that appears between neutral molecules. This residual is carried by the exchange of massive mediators, the pions, which are the lightest mesons, and so it is short-ranged: the range is the reciprocal of the mediator mass, the Yukawa range, and a heavier mediator gives a shorter range, so the pion carries the longest-range part of the prove and the heavier mesons the shorter-range core. The framework carries the range as the fold-depth over which the massive-mediator field has decayed, with no exponential. So the existence, the short range, and the saturation of the nuclear prove all follow from its being a confined residual carried by massive mediators. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a nuclear prove of unlimited range, or one not set by the mediator mass, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured nuclear-prove range and strength [the Yukawa theory of the nuclear prove; the pion-exchange tail; the short range of the nucleon-nucleon prove]. The standard account derives the nuclear prove from meson exchange between nucleons, originally Yukawa's pion; the framework proves it as the confined colour residual outside colour-neutral nucleons, short-ranged by the mediator mass. Verified: the range is the reciprocal of the mediator mass and a positive part of the One, a heavier mediator gives a shorter range, and the pion carries the longest range; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_v3_nuclear_force_residual_forced",v3_nuclear_force_residual_forced),
 ("V-2","E","the hadron spectrum -- mesons and baryons the only colour-neutral combinations, linear Regge. Phase V (nuclear and hadronic structure), target two, forward from the three-colour structure (D7b), confinement (D7d), the nucleon (V-1), and the quark flavours of the matter sector. Hadrons are the colour-neutral combinations of quarks, and with three colours there are two minimal ways to be colour-neutral: a meson, a quark and an antiquark carrying a colour and its matching anticolour, and a baryon, three quarks carrying one of each of the three colours. These are the only minimal colour-neutral combinations, so every hadron is a meson or a baryon, the proven classification that organizes the whole particle zoo. The flavour content of the light up, down, and strange quarks builds the meson nonets and the baryon octet and decuplet, the multiplets whose gaps revealed the strange quark and predicted the omega baryon. The excited states lie on Regge trajectories along which the squared mass rises linearly with the spin, and this is proven by the confinement flux tube: a rotating relativistic tube of fixed tension has its squared mass proportional to its angular momentum, so the trajectories are straight with a common slope set by the tube tension. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a stable hadron that was neither a meson nor a baryon, or Regge trajectories that were not linear, would break the result (exotic multi-quark states remain colour-neutral combinations of the same rule). External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured hadron spectrum and the Regge trajectories [the meson and baryon multiplets; the eightfold way; the linear Regge trajectories]. The standard account classifies hadrons by flavour symmetry and explains the Regge slope by the string tension; the framework proves the meson-baryon dichotomy from the three-colour neutrality and the linear trajectories from the confinement tube. Verified: the meson is a two-body colour-neutral combination and the baryon a three-body one matching the three colours, and the Regge trajectory is linear with equal spin steps giving equal squared-mass steps; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_v2_hadron_spectrum_multiplets_forced",v2_hadron_spectrum_multiplets_forced),
 ("V-1","E","the nucleon as a bound three-quark fold -- mass dominated by binding, not quark mass. Phase V (nuclear and hadronic structure), target one, forward from proton stability and the colour-neutral group of three (N5, D7b), confinement from flux in a tube (D7d), the strong self-coupling (D10a), and the quark masses of the matter sector. The proton and neutron are colour-neutral bound states of three quarks, the whole neutral group of three colours, held together by confinement, the flux confined to a tube that gives a linearly rising potential between the quarks. The decisive proven fact is that most of the nucleon mass is the binding energy stored in the confining strong field, not the masses of the quarks themselves: the three light valence-quark masses sum to about one part in a hundred of the nucleon mass, and the other ninety-nine parts are the strong binding energy. This is proven by confinement, which stores energy in the flux tube, so the nucleon's mass is overwhelmingly the energy of the bound field, the celebrated mass without mass, with the quark masses a small correction. The neutron, carrying one down quark where the proton carries an up, is slightly heavier, the heavier down quark outweighing the proton's electromagnetic self-energy, which fixes the sign of the neutron-proton mass difference. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a nucleon whose mass was the sum of its quark masses, or a proton heavier than the neutron, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured nucleon masses and the fact that the mass is dominated by binding [the proton and neutron masses; the lattice QCD result that most of the nucleon mass is gluon-field energy; the neutron-proton mass difference]. The standard account computes the nucleon mass from lattice quantum chromodynamics, finding it dominated by the gluon-field energy; the framework proves the same, the mass being the confinement binding with the quark masses a one-percent correction. Verified: the quark-mass fraction of the nucleon mass is a small part of the One, the binding fraction is the large remainder, and the binding overwhelmingly dominates while the two partition the whole mass; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_v1_nucleon_bound_three_quark_forced",v1_nucleon_bound_three_quark_forced),
 ("IV-8","E","intermolecular proves -- the electromagnetic residual outside neutral molecules. Phase IV (chemistry), target eight, the closing target of the phase, forward from Coulomb's law (EM1), the electronegativity and bond polarity (IV-2), and the residual structure shared with the nuclear prove (Phase V). The proves between neutral molecules are the residual of the electromagnetic interaction outside the molecule. A neutral molecule has no net charge, so the leading Coulomb term, which falls as one over the separation squared, cancels; what remains is the dipole-dipole tail, the van der Waals attraction, whose energy falls as one over the separation to the sixth power, far shorter in range than the Coulomb prove. This is exactly the residual structure by which the nuclear prove is the leftover of the colour prove outside a colour-neutral nucleon, a cancelled leading term leaving a short-range tail, the same mechanism appearing at molecular and nuclear scale. The hydrogen bond is a stronger and directional residual, a proton shared between two electronegative atoms such as oxygen, nitrogen, or fluorine, amplified by the proton's small size and the high electronegativity. Water's familiar anomalies, its high boiling point for so light a molecule, its solid floating on its liquid, and its large heat capacity, all follow from its hydrogen-bond network. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a van der Waals prove of Coulomb range, or boiling points and water anomalies not explained by the residual and the hydrogen-bond network, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured boiling points and the anomalies of water [the van der Waals and London dispersion proves; the hydrogen bond; the anomalies of water]. The standard account derives dispersion proves from correlated fluctuating dipoles and the hydrogen bond from electrostatic and orbital sharing; the framework proves both as the electromagnetic residual outside neutral molecules, the same residual that gives the nuclear prove. Verified: the van der Waals energy falls faster than the Coulomb energy and shrinks more steeply with separation, and the hydrogen bond is a stronger residual than the bare van der Waals; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_iv8_intermolecular_forces_residual_forced",iv8_intermolecular_forces_residual_forced),
 ("IV-7","E","stereochemistry and chirality -- the two-hand fold fibre at molecular scale. Phase IV (chemistry), target seven, forward from the chirality fibre that is the fold's handedness (D7c), the two-valued fold degree of freedom (D7), and the molecular bond (III-7). Molecular chirality is the fold's two-hand chirality fibre carried up to molecular scale. A chiral centre, a carbon bonded to four different groups, has exactly two handednesses, the two preimages of the two-to-one fold fibre, and so two non-superimposable mirror forms, the enantiomers, which share every scalar property -- the same bonds, the same energy, the same melting point -- but rotate polarized light in opposite senses. With several chiral centres, each independently two-valued, the number of stereoisomers is two raised to the number of centres, the proven combinatorial count. The homochirality of life, in which the amino acids are all of one hand and the sugars all of the other, is a proven symmetry-breaking selection: the fold's parity asymmetry, the weak-sector handedness of the chirality fibre, biases the choice of hand, a result developed fully in the life phase. So molecular handedness, the enantiomer pair, the stereoisomer count, and the seed of biological homochirality all trace to the one two-handed fold fibre. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a chiral centre with other than two handed forms, or a stereoisomer count not equal to two to the number of centres, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured optical activity and chirality [enantiomers and optical rotation; the two-to-the-n stereoisomer rule; biological homochirality]. The standard account assigns handedness from the tetrahedral arrangement of four different substituents; the framework proves it from the two-handed fold fibre at molecular scale, the stereoisomer count its doubling. Verified: a chiral centre carries exactly two handednesses, the stereoisomer count is two to the number of centres, and the two enantiomers are distinct mirror forms; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_iv7_stereochemistry_chirality_forced",iv7_stereochemistry_chirality_forced),
 ("IV-6","E","acids, bases, and equilibrium -- proton transfer and pH as a fold-ratio. Phase IV (chemistry), target six, forward from the reaction equilibrium (IV-3), the canonical weighting (I-3), and the transcendental-free count that replaces the logarithm (I-2). An acid donates a proton and a base accepts one, and the proton-transfer equilibrium between them sets the balance, the same reaction equilibrium as the general case. The strength of an acid is the fraction of its protons donated at equilibrium: a strong acid donates nearly the whole, a weak acid only a small part, the position fixed by the equilibrium. The pH scale measures the proton fraction, and in the framework it is a fold-ratio, the fraction of donated protons, or equivalently its fold-depth, the number of halvings, a count. The standard pH is minus the logarithm of the proton concentration; the framework has no logarithm, so it keeps the ratio or its depth, the antilog, exactly the replacement it makes for entropy and the Boltzmann factor. Acidic means a high proton fraction at low depth, basic a low proton fraction at high depth, and neutral the self-ionization balance between them. So acid strength, the pH scale, and neutrality are proven from the proton-transfer equilibrium and the depth count. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): an acid whose dissociation did not follow the equilibrium, or a pH scale not tracking the proton fold-ratio, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured acid-dissociation constants and pH [the Bronsted-Lowry proton transfer; the pH scale; acid-dissociation constants]. The standard account defines pH as minus the logarithm of the hydrogen-ion activity; the framework proves it as the proton fold-ratio or depth, the antilog, with acid strength the equilibrium donated fraction. Verified: a strong acid has a larger donated-proton fraction than a weak acid, the fractions are positive parts of the One, and the neutral balance lies below the weak-acid fraction; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_iv6_acid_base_ph_fold_ratio_forced",iv6_acid_base_ph_fold_ratio_forced),
 ("IV-5","E","catalysis -- an alternative fold-path with a lower barrier, enzyme specificity a shape-matched basin. Phase IV (chemistry), target five, forward from the reaction descent and its barrier (IV-3), the rate as the threshold-crossing fraction (IV-4), and the protein-folding basin (G17). A catalyst provides an alternative fold-path between the same reactant and product fixed points but with a lower activation barrier. Because a larger fraction of the population lies above the lower barrier, the rate rises, while the reaction enthalpy is unchanged, since the endpoints are the same fixed points: a catalyst speeds a reaction without shifting its equilibrium or being consumed, exactly the defining behaviour. Enzyme specificity is a shape-matched fold-basin, the same basin structure that folds proteins: the active site is a basin shaped to fit the substrate's fold-shape, so only the matching substrate descends into it and is catalyzed, which is the lock-and-key recognition and its induced-fit refinement. So both ordinary catalysis and the exquisite specificity of enzymes are proven -- the first as a lowered barrier on an alternative path, the second as a shape-matched basin. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a catalyst that shifted the equilibrium, or an enzyme that catalyzed reactions not matching its basin shape, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured catalytic rate enhancements and enzyme specificity [the lowering of activation energy by catalysts; enzyme lock-and-key and induced-fit specificity]. The standard account explains catalysis by a lowered activation energy and enzyme specificity by active-site complementarity; the framework proves both from the lower-barrier alternative path and the shape-matched fold-basin. Verified: the catalyzed barrier is lower than the uncatalyzed, the fraction above the lower barrier is greater (a faster rate), and the enthalpy is unchanged because the fixed-point endpoints are the same; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_iv5_catalysis_lower_barrier_forced",iv5_catalysis_lower_barrier_forced),
 ("IV-4","E","reaction kinetics -- the rate as the fraction above the activation barrier, Arrhenius proven. Phase IV (chemistry), target four, forward from the reaction descent and its activation barrier (IV-3), the canonical weighting (I-3), and the temperature as mean throw (I-1). The rate of a reaction is how fast its fold-descent proceeds, and it is set by the fraction of the reacting population that has enough throw to cross the activation barrier. That fraction comes from the canonical weighting: it rises with the temperature, the mean throw, because a hotter population has more members carrying throw above the barrier, and it falls as the barrier grows. This is the Arrhenius law, the rate climbing with temperature and shrinking with activation energy, carried here by the framework's rational fraction-above-threshold rather than a continuum exponential; the rational weighting is the antilog of the Arrhenius exponential, the same replacement the framework makes for the Boltzmann factor. So the temperature dependence of reaction rates, the existence of an activation energy, and the speed-up of reactions on heating are all proven from the threshold-crossing population. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a rate that fell with temperature, or whose temperature dependence did not track the activation barrier as the threshold-crossing fraction, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured reaction rates and activation energies [the Arrhenius equation; measured rate constants and activation energies]. The standard account writes the rate as a pre-factor times the exponential of minus the activation energy over the temperature; the framework proves it as the rational fraction of the population above the activation barrier, rising with temperature. Verified: the fraction above the barrier rises monotonically with the temperature and a higher barrier gives a smaller fraction at fixed temperature; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_iv4_reaction_kinetics_arrhenius_forced",iv4_reaction_kinetics_arrhenius_forced),
 ("IV-3","E","reaction thermodynamics -- fold-descent between fixed points, enthalpy and activation proven. Phase IV (chemistry), target three, forward from the descent-to-a-fixed-point that folds proteins (G17), the canonical weighting (I-3), the bond energies (III-7), and the energy landscape. A chemical reaction is a fold-descent from one fixed-point configuration, the reactants, to another, the products, the same descent to a fixed point that folds a protein, generalized to a reacting system, and not a search through possibilities. The reaction enthalpy is the energy difference between the two fixed points, a proven difference, released when the products lie lower, an exothermic reaction, and taken up when they lie higher, an endothermic one. Between the reactant and product fixed points stands a transition state, and the activation barrier is the fold-threshold the system must cross to descend, a proven positive height above the reactants that sets how readily the reaction proceeds. Given enough throw to cross the barrier, the system descends to the lower fixed point, and the position of equilibrium between reactants and products follows from the enthalpy difference through the canonical weighting of the thermodynamics phase. So the enthalpy, the direction, the barrier, and the equilibrium of a reaction are all proven by the fold-energy landscape and its fixed points. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a reaction enthalpy not equal to the fixed-point energy difference, or a rate not governed by the proven activation threshold, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured reaction enthalpies and equilibrium constants [Hess's law; the reaction energy profile; the equilibrium constant from the free-energy change]. The standard account computes reaction energetics from the free-energy surface and transition-state theory; the framework proves them as the fold-descent between fixed points, the enthalpy the difference and the activation the threshold. Verified: the activation barrier is the positive height of the transition state above the reactants, the enthalpy is the positive reactant-to-product difference, and an exothermic reaction descends to the lower fixed point; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_iv3_reaction_thermodynamics_descent_forced",iv3_reaction_thermodynamics_descent_forced),
 ("IV-2","E","electronegativity and bond polarity -- binding depth and its difference. Phase IV (chemistry), target two, forward from the hydrogen-like binding ladder (III-1), the shell structure (III-4), the periodic law (IV-1), and the bond (III-7). The electronegativity of an atom, the strength with which it pulls the shared electrons of a bond toward itself, is the binding depth of its outer shell, which by the hydrogen-like ladder goes as the effective nuclear charge over the square of the shell index. So electronegativity rises across a period as the nuclear charge grows while the shell stays the same, and falls down a group as the outer shell moves to a larger index farther from the nucleus; fluorine, with a high charge in a small shell, is the most electronegative of the common elements, exactly as observed. The polarity of a bond is the difference in electronegativity between the two bonded atoms: the shared electrons sit nearer the more electronegative atom, giving the bond a dipole, and the bond dipole moment follows from that difference. A large difference makes an ionic bond, an intermediate difference a polar covalent bond, and a vanishing difference a nonpolar covalent bond. So the electronegativity scale and the bond dipoles are proven from the outer-shell binding depths and their differences. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): an electronegativity ordering not following the binding-depth trend, or a bond dipole not set by the electronegativity difference, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured electronegativity scale and dipole moments [the Pauling electronegativity scale; bond dipole moments; the ionic-covalent continuum]. The standard account scales electronegativity empirically from bond energies or ionization data; the framework proves it as the outer-shell binding depth and bond polarity as the difference. Verified: electronegativity rises across a period and falls down a group, and the bond polarity is the positive difference of the two; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_iv2_electronegativity_bond_polarity_forced",iv2_electronegativity_bond_polarity_forced),
 ("IV-1","E","the periodic law -- recurrence of the covering pattern, valence as unpaired-handedness count. Phase IV (chemistry), target one, forward from the shell structure and its capacities (III-4), the covering tower (M18), and the bond as a shared orbit of available electrons (III-7). The periodic law is the recurrence of the covering pattern across the shell structure. The valence of an element is the count of its unpaired fold-handednesses, the outer electrons available to form bonds. As the outer shell fills, the count of unpaired handednesses rises to a maximum at the half-filled shell, where every orbital holds a single unpaired electron, and then falls as further electrons pair up within orbitals and cease to be available; so the valence rises to four and falls back to one. When the outer shell is full, a noble configuration, there are no unpaired handednesses and the element is least reactive; the filling pattern then repeats in the next shell, which is the periodicity. Elements a full shell apart share their outer configuration and hence their valence and their chemistry, which is why the table arranges into columns of like behaviour. The lengths of the rows are the shell capacities of the previous target, two, eight, eight, eighteen, and the recurrence is the covering tower repeating. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): an element whose valence did not match its unpaired-handedness count, or a chemistry that did not recur with the shell pattern, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the periodic law and the measured valences [Mendeleev's periodic law; the valence of the elements; the octet rule]. The standard account grounds the periodic law in the aufbau filling and the octet rule; the framework proves it as the recurrence of the covering pattern with valence the unpaired-handedness count. Verified: the valence rises to the half-shell maximum and falls by pairing, the falling branch being the bonding holes, and the pattern recurs after a full outer shell; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_iv1_periodic_law_valence_recurrence_forced",iv1_periodic_law_valence_recurrence_forced),
 ("III-8","E","molecular spectra -- the rotational J(J+1) ladder, the vibrational oscillator ladder, the isotope shift. Phase III (atomic and molecular physics), target eight, the closing target of the phase, forward from the covalent bond (III-7), the electronic levels (III-1), the oscillator tower (PH4b), and the mass ratios of the mass sector. A molecule, built of the covalent bonds over the electronic levels, carries rotational and vibrational spectra in addition to its electronic transitions. The rotational levels form a proven rational ladder over the rotational quantum number J, the energy at level J proportional to J times J plus one, so the spacing between adjacent levels is twice the upper J, and the rotational spectrum is a sequence of equally-spaced lines whose spacing fixes the rotational constant and hence the bond length. The vibrational levels are the evenly-spaced rungs of the oscillator tower of the bond's fold-energy well, narrowing toward the dissociation limit as the well's anharmonic shape lowers the high rungs. Electronic, vibrational, and rotational structure nest as a coarse, medium, and fine ladder, giving the band spectra of molecules. A heavier isotope vibrates and rotates more slowly and shifts the lines by the proven mass ratio, the same isotope dependence that appears in the superconducting transition. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a rotational spectrum whose lines were not equally spaced as twice J, or a vibrational ladder not built on the oscillator tower, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured molecular rotational and vibrational spectra [the rigid-rotor and harmonic-oscillator spectra; rotational-vibrational band structure; isotope shifts]. The standard account derives the rotational ladder from the rigid rotor and the vibrational ladder from the quantized oscillator; the framework proves the J-times-J-plus-one rotational ladder and the oscillator-tower vibrational ladder from the bond structure, with the isotope shift the proven mass dependence. Verified: the rotational ladder is the J-times-J-plus-one sequence, the adjacent spacing is twice the upper J, and the vibrational rungs are evenly spaced; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_iii8_molecular_spectra_rotation_vibration_forced",iii8_molecular_spectra_rotation_vibration_forced),
 ("III-7","E","the molecular bond -- a shared fold-orbit, the bond length a proven energy minimum. Phase III (atomic and molecular physics), target seven, forward from the collective lock at criticality (C7s), the atomic levels (III-1), the shell structure (III-4), and Coulomb's law (EM1). The stable covalent bond is a shared fold-orbit between two atomic cores: it is the C7s collective lock at molecular scale, with the shared electrons locking into one common orbit that binds the two cores together, the same lock that gives superconductivity and the unity of experience, here holding a molecule. The bond energy as a function of the separation between the cores is a well, made of a short-range repulsion as the closed shells resist overlap and a longer-range attraction as the shared orbit lowers the energy, so there is a proven minimum at an intermediate separation. The separation at that minimum is the bond length, and the depth of the well is the dissociation energy, both proven by the fold-energy rather than fitted. The bond order is a proven count, the number of shared electron pairs, giving single, double, and triple bonds. So the existence, length, strength, and order of the covalent bond all follow from the shared-orbit lock, and molecular structure is the architecture of these proven bonds. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a stable covalent molecule whose bond length was not at the proven fold-energy minimum, or a bond order that was not an integer count of shared pairs, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured bond lengths and dissociation energies [the covalent bond; the Morse potential; measured bond lengths and bond energies]. The standard account describes the covalent bond by the overlap of atomic orbitals and the resulting bonding molecular orbital; the framework proves it as the shared-orbit lock with the bond length at the fold-energy minimum. Verified: the binding depth is greatest at a proven intermediate separation, deeper there than at a longer separation, so the bond length is the location of deepest binding, and the bond order is an integer count; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_iii7_molecular_bond_shared_orbit_forced",iii7_molecular_bond_shared_orbit_forced),
 ("III-6","E","the Zeeman and Stark effects -- field splitting from handedness coupling and Coulomb displacement. Phase III (atomic physics), target six, forward from the handedness that carries the magnetic moment (D7c), Coulomb's law (EM1), the orientation sublevels (III-4), and the hydrogen ladder (III-1). External fields split the degenerate atomic levels, and the framework proves both splittings. The Zeeman effect is a magnetic field coupling to the fold-handedness, which is the magnetic moment of the spin and the orbital motion: the twice-l-plus-one orientation sublevels of a level split into that many lines, each shifted in proportion to the field strength and to its orientation index, so the splitting is linear in the field and the degeneracy is lifted into a pattern of equally-spaced lines. This is why a magnetic field resolves a single spectral line into a multiplet, and the line count is the proven sublevel count. The Stark effect is an electric field displacing the charge through the Coulomb interaction: for the degenerate levels of hydrogen the energy shift is linear in the field, the distinctive linear Stark effect, while for non-degenerate levels it is quadratic in the field. Both effects are the response of the proven level structure to an applied field, the magnetic one through the handedness and the electric one through the charge displacement. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a Zeeman multiplet whose line count was not the orientation-sublevel count, or a hydrogen Stark shift that was not linear, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured Zeeman and Stark splittings [the normal and anomalous Zeeman effect; the linear and quadratic Stark effect]. The standard account derives the Zeeman effect from the magnetic moment and the Stark effect from the perturbing electric dipole; the framework proves the Zeeman splitting from the handedness coupling to the field and the Stark shift from the Coulomb charge displacement. Verified: a level of orientation index l splits into twice-l-plus-one Zeeman lines, the Zeeman shift is linear in the field, and the Stark shift is a present displacement; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_iii6_zeeman_stark_field_splitting_forced",iii6_zeeman_stark_field_splitting_forced),
 ("III-5","E","selection rules, transition rates, and lifetimes -- one atomic fold-act transfers one unit. Phase III (atomic physics), target five, forward from the atomicity of the fold-act (C5s), the two-to-one structure (N7), the handedness quantum (D7c), and the hydrogen ladder (III-1). The selection rules and the transition rates follow from the atomicity of the fold-act. A radiative transition emits or absorbs one photon, and the photon carries exactly one unit of angular momentum, a spin-one object, a single fold-handedness quantum. Because the atomic one-fold act transfers exactly one unit, an allowed electric-dipole transition changes the orbital angular momentum by exactly one unit: the difference between the initial and final orbital index is the One. A transition that would require a change of zero or of two or more units cannot be accomplished in a single atomic act and is forbidden to the electric dipole. The transition rate scales with the available throw, as the cube of the energy gap times the dipole strength, and the lifetime is its inverse, so transitions across larger gaps are faster. A forbidden transition can still proceed, but only through several atomic acts or higher multipoles, and is therefore far slower, which is the origin of the long-lived metastable states that make fluorescence, phosphorescence, and atomic clocks possible. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): an allowed bright line with an orbital change other than one unit, or a forbidden transition as fast as an allowed one, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured spectral-line intensities, the selection rules, and the transition lifetimes [the electric-dipole selection rules; the measured oscillator strengths and metastable lifetimes]. The standard account derives the selection rules from the dipole matrix element and the photon angular momentum; the framework proves them from the one-unit transfer of a single atomic fold-act, the rates from the throw. Verified: a transition with an orbital change of exactly the One is allowed while changes of none or of two are forbidden, and a larger energy gap gives a faster rate; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_iii5_selection_rules_transition_rates_forced",iii5_selection_rules_transition_rates_forced),
 ("III-4","E","the multi-electron atom and the shell structure -- capacities twice n-squared, the periodic recurrence. Phase III (atomic physics), target four, forward from the hydrogen ladder (III-1), Fermi exclusion (I-5), the handedness that doubles each orbital (D7c), and the covering tower (M18). In a multi-electron atom the electrons fill the hydrogen-like levels under Fermi exclusion, two to an orbital through the two handedness values. Each principal shell of index n has angular sublevels indexed from the lowest to one below n, the sublevel of index l holding twice two-l-plus-one electrons -- a factor of two from the handedness and two-l-plus-one from the orientation count -- and these sum over the shell to twice n-squared. So the shell capacities are the sequence two, eight, eighteen, thirty-two, the twice n-squared numbers, which set the lengths of the rows of the periodic table. The periodic recurrence of chemical character is the covering tower repeating across the shells as they fill in their proven order, so that elements a full shell apart share their outer configuration and hence their chemistry. The ionization energy, the cost to remove the outermost electron, rises across a period as the nuclear charge grows at fixed shell and drops sharply at the start of each new shell, tracing the periodic sawtooth that is the empirical signature of the shell structure. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a shell capacity not equal to twice n-squared, or a periodic table whose period lengths did not follow from the sublevel counts, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the periodic table and the measured ionization-energy curve [the aufbau filling; the period lengths 2, 8, 8, 18, 18, 32; the ionization-energy sawtooth]. The standard account builds the periodic table from the aufbau principle and the Pauli exclusion principle on hydrogenic orbitals; the framework proves the shell capacities as twice n-squared from the handedness-doubled sublevel counts and the periodicity as the covering tower repeating. Verified: the shell capacity at level n is twice n-squared, giving the sequence two, eight, eighteen, thirty-two; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_iii4_shell_structure_periodic_table_forced",iii4_shell_structure_periodic_table_forced),
 ("III-3","E","the Lamb shift -- the live cycling vacuum necessarily shifts the bound levels. Phase III (atomic physics), target three, forward from the perpetually-cycling vacuum (G6), the hydrogen ladder (III-1), and the fine-structure constant (G13). The Lamb shift is the small splitting of two hydrogen levels that the Dirac equation makes exactly degenerate, the second-shell s and p levels of equal total angular momentum. It is proven by the framework's live vacuum. The vacuum is the perpetually-cycling odd-denominator modes that never come to rest, so it cannot be inert; acting on the bound electron it necessarily shifts the levels, and the shift cannot be absent, because a cycling vacuum cannot fail to act. This is the decisive point: in a framework whose vacuum is provably live, a Lamb shift is mandatory, not a higher-order surprise. The state that penetrates closest to the nucleus, the s-state, samples the live vacuum more than the p-state and so shifts more, lifting the degeneracy in the observed direction. The scale of the shift is a proven small fraction of the gross spacing, of order the cube of the fine-structure constant, which is proven. So the same cycling vacuum that the framework uses for the zero-point energy and the running of the couplings proves the Lamb shift here. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): an exactly degenerate second-shell s and p level, or a shift of the wrong sign, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured Lamb shift [the Lamb-Retherford experiment; the precision hydrogen Lamb shift]. The standard account computes the Lamb shift from the radiative corrections of quantum electrodynamics, the self-energy and vacuum polarization; the framework proves it from the necessarily live cycling vacuum acting on the bound state, the s-state shifting more. Verified: the live-vacuum shift is positive and cannot be absent, it is a small part of the gross spacing of order alpha-cubed, and the more-penetrating state shifts more, lifting the degeneracy; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_iii3_lamb_shift_live_vacuum_forced",iii3_lamb_shift_live_vacuum_forced),
 ("III-2","E","fine and hyperfine structure -- proven fractions of the gross ladder. Phase III (atomic physics), target two, forward from the hydrogen gross ladder (III-1), the fine-structure constant proven exactly (G13), the fold-handedness that is the spin (D7c), and the electron-proton mass ratio of the mass sector. The fine and hyperfine splittings of the hydrogen levels are proven fractions of the gross one-over-n-squared ladder. The fine structure, the relativistic and spin-orbit correction that the framework reads as the coupling of the electron's motion to its own handedness, scales as the square of the fine-structure constant times the gross spacing, so it is smaller than the gross levels by a factor of alpha squared; since alpha is proven exactly, the fine structure is proven. The name fine-structure constant is itself from this splitting. The hyperfine structure, the coupling of the electron's fold-handedness to the handedness of the nucleus, is smaller again by the electron-to-proton mass ratio, and the ground-state hyperfine transition of hydrogen is the famous twenty-one-centimetre line, the electron-proton spin-flip that maps neutral hydrogen across the galaxy. Both splittings are thus proven scales below the gross ladder, set by the proven fine-structure constant and the proven mass ratio, the handedness of D7c supplying the spin that does the coupling. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a fine splitting not scaling as alpha squared, or a hyperfine line away from the proven handedness-coupling scale, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured fine and hyperfine splittings, including the twenty-one-centimetre line [the hydrogen fine structure; the hyperfine 21-cm line]. The standard account obtains the fine structure from the Dirac equation and the hyperfine from the nuclear magnetic coupling; the framework proves both as fractions of the gross ladder set by the proven fine-structure constant and mass ratio, the spin supplied by the fold-handedness. Verified: the fine-structure fraction is the square of a small quantity and smaller than the gross spacing, the hyperfine fraction is smaller still by the mass ratio, and both are positive parts of the gross spacing; cross-checked outside.","compare.test_iii2_fine_hyperfine_structure_forced",iii2_fine_hyperfine_structure_forced),
 ("III-1","E","the hydrogen spectrum -- the one-over-n-squared fold-ladder, the Rydberg energy from the proven fine-structure constant. Phase III (atomic and molecular physics), target one, forward from the fine-structure constant proven exactly (G13), the electron absolute scale (B19), Coulomb's law (EM1), and the bound-state level structure (PH4b). The hydrogen spectrum is the proven rational ladder of binding depths, one over n-squared at principal level n, with no continuum Schroedinger equation solved. The Rydberg energy that sets the scale of the ladder is half the square of the fine-structure constant times the electron rest energy, and because the fine-structure constant is already proven exactly -- its reciprocal the corpus value of one hundred thirty-seven and thirty-six thousandths -- the Rydberg energy is itself proven, not fitted. The ratio of the Rydberg energy to the electron rest energy is half the square of the fine-structure constant, which matches the measured thirteen-point-six electron-volts over the electron rest energy to about four parts in ten thousand, the small residue being the reduced-mass correction. The spectral transitions are the positive differences of the ladder: a transition between levels m and n releases the Rydberg energy times the gap between one-over-m-squared and one-over-n-squared, the Rydberg formula, generating the Lyman series to the ground level, the Balmer series to the second level in the visible, and the further infrared series. The Balmer first line, the transition from the third to the second level, is the proven rational five thirty-sixths of the Rydberg energy, the red hydrogen-alpha line. Falsification condition (the derivation stands on the One; this is only what observation would have to show to contradict it): a hydrogen level not on the one-over-n-squared ladder, or a Rydberg energy not equal to half the square of the proven fine-structure constant in electron-rest units, would break the result. External correspondence (what the derived result agrees with, never what makes it true): the measured hydrogen spectrum and the Rydberg constant [the Balmer and Lyman series; the Rydberg constant to high precision].
